coredata.dc:title: Delignified wood for thermal energy storage with high efficient photo-thermal conversion efficiency
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yanchen
Lu, Qi
Yang, Jiawei
He, Weiwei
coredata.dc:description: Wood is widely used in human life as a natural renewable material. Improving the temperature regulation function of wood is of great significance to reduce the consumption of fossil energy and promote the construction of a low-carbon society. In this study, a phase change energy storage wood (PCES-Wood) with efficient photo-heat conversion efficiency was obtained by impregnating polyethylene glycol based composite material and graphene quantum dot grafted boron nitride (GQDs-BN) as thermal conductive filler. The results show that the thermal stability and the dispersion of GQDs-BN are improved. GQDs-BN shows strong light absorption in both UV and visible segments, and the thermal conductivity exhibited by PCES-Wood reaches 0.3916 W/(m·K) with the GQDs-BN content reaching 6 %. The phase transition temperature of PCES-Wood is between 20.29 °C and 36.29 °C, and the melting and solidification enthalpies reach 114.90 J/g and 113.70 J/g, respectively, reflecting a high latent heat value and a range suitable for human comfort temperature. 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa also shows a low leakage rate as only 0.70 % at high temperature. After simulating sunlight exposure, the surface temperature of GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa increases rapidly and the heat storage capacity is persistent under low-temperature conditions. The PCES-Wood prepared in this study enhances the potential of wood application in the field of temperature regulation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wood
Heat adjustment
Photothermal conversion
Interface modification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical properties and dispersibility of GQDs-BN

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Postdoctoral Innovation Talents Support Program ( BX20230185 ), the Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation ( 3244042 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of BN and GQDs-BN. (b) XPS peaks of BN, OH-BN, KH550-BN and GQDs-BN. (c) C element peak of BN. (d) O peak of BN. (e) N peak of BN. (f) The C peak of OH-BN. (g) O peak of OH-BN. (h) N element peak of OH-BN. (i) C peak of KH550-BN. (j) O of KH550-BN Element peaks. (k) N peaks of KH550-BN. (l) C element peaks of GQDs-BN. (m) O peaks of GQDs-BN. (n) N element peaks of GQDs-BN.
(a) Raman spectra and (b) XRD pattern of BN, OH-BN, KH550-BN and GQDs-BN. (c) Ultraviolet-visible spectrum and (d) TGA curves of BN and GQDs-BN.
Dispersion of BN (a) in water and (b) in PEG 400. Dispersion of GQDs-BN (c) in water and (d) in PEG.
(a) GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa microstructure. (b) Infrared spectra of 70PEG1000-Balsa, 6 % GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa, and 6 %GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa following 500 thermal cycles. (c) XRD patterns of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa.
(a) DSC heating process, (b) DSC cooling process, (c) Supercooling degree, and (d) heat loss rate of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa.

(a) DSC heating process of 6 % GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa before and after 200 cycles. (b) DSC cooling curves of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa before and after 200 cycles. (c) TGA curves of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa. (d) DTG curves of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa.
(a) Ultraviolet-visible spectrum and (b) Photothermal conversion curve of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa.
Thermal conductivity of Balsa, 70PEG1000-Balsa and GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa.
PTT and PTE of GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa.

head.ce:title: Delignified wood for thermal energy storage with high efficient photo-thermal conversion efficiency

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Future Laboratory, Tsinghua University, 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Future Laboratory, Tsinghua University, 100084, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Arts & Design, Tsinghua University, 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Arts & Design, Tsinghua University,100084, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: The Future Laboratory, Tsinghua University, 100084, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wood is widely used in human life as a natural renewable material. Improving the temperature regulation function of wood is of great significance to reduce the consumption of fossil energy and promote the construction of a low-carbon society. In this study, a phase change energy storage wood (PCES-Wood) with efficient photo-heat conversion efficiency was obtained by impregnating polyethylene glycol based composite material and graphene quantum dot grafted boron nitride (GQDs-BN) as thermal conductive filler. The results show that the thermal stability and the dispersion of GQDs-BN are improved. GQDs-BN shows strong light absorption in both UV and visible segments, and the thermal conductivity exhibited by PCES-Wood reaches 0.3916 W/(m·K) with the GQDs-BN content reaching 6 %. The phase transition temperature of PCES-Wood is between 20.29 °C and 36.29 °C, and the melting and solidification enthalpies reach 114.90 J/g and 113.70 J/g, respectively, reflecting a high latent heat value and a range suitable for human comfort temperature. 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa also shows a low leakage rate as only 0.70 % at high temperature. After simulating sunlight exposure, the surface temperature of GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa increases rapidly and the heat storage capacity is persistent under low-temperature conditions. The PCES-Wood prepared in this study enhances the potential of wood application in the field of temperature regulation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The survival and development of human society cannot do without energy at all times [1]. The establishment of the industrial social system and the fast development of science and technology are accompanied by human's increasing demand for energy [2]. People have increasingly higher requirements for new green energy and energy efficiency improvement. In this case, energy storage technology has emerged, which is capable of reducing industrial production energy consumption, recovering industrial waste heat, as well as transferring excess energy from the low heat utility period to the peak heat utility period. It can also absorb solar energy and geothermal energy to reduce the dependence of human society on fossil energy [3,4].

Phase change material (PCM) refers to a kind of materials capable of changing the physical state (solid-liquid, liquid-gas, solid-gas, solid-solid, etc.) with the temperature, light, or chemical energy as the driving force to realize the heat absorption and release function [5]. In the process of phase change, the chemical properties of the material, temperature can be small change [6]. Among them, organic solid-liquid PCM exhibit high energy storage density, low price, environmental protection and no pollution, which have gradually become a hot topic in the field of new energy research [7]. However, there is leakage in the phase change process of such materials, which largely restricts its further development. For this defect, the flow of PCM can be constrained by the encapsulating material so that the solid-state morphology of the composite does not change [8]. Among encapsulating materials, wood is a kind of renewable material with good packaging performance, which has a large capillary and micro capillary structure composed of catheter, axial tube cell, pores and so on, which can be used as a matrix material to provide the possibility for the packaging of PCM to form phase change energy storage wood (PCES-Wood) [9–11]. Besides, the rich chemical reactivity exhibited by the wood surface can improve the dimensional stability with PCM through esterification, self-assembly and self-polymerization [12,13].

At present, the research on PCES-Wood is still in its initial stage, and the existing research reports can be divided into four aspects: (1) the natural pore structure of wood is used to physically adsorb the PCM. For example, Li et al. [14] made the first attempt to package polyethylene glycol (PEG) in unmodified fir wood to obtain the PCES-Wood with a phase change temperature (PCT) of 26.74 °C, but the packaging capacity of unmodified fir was poor. (2) Wood is delignification treatment to improve the packaging amount of PCM. For example, Yang et al. [15] delaminated and carbonized the plane wood to PCES-Wood. Dodecane acid was filled by up to 81.1 %, and the capillary force of the wood limited the loss of dodecane acid. For PCES-Wood, the PCT and latent heat were 41.0 °C and 177.9 J/g during melting, and 38.9 °C and 178.2 J/g during solidification, respectively. However, if the PCT was too high, it was not suitable for the daily environment. Ma et al. [16] treated lignin and then impregnated with dismalic acid-palmitate binary co-melting PCM. The results showed that PCM and wood were physical binding, and the immersion rate of PCES-Wood reached 61.2 %. The PCT was 23.4 °C and the latent heat value was 94.4 J/g, making it suitable for daily use environment. (3) The synthesis of PCM in wood to enhance the dimensional stability of PCES-Wood. For example, Xu et al. [17] synthesized polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based silica sol in wood by sol-gel method, and formed SiO2 network in modified wood for preventing PEG leakage. The latent heat value of PCES-Wood was 46.7 J/g, which showed good thermal stability and excellent dimensional stability. Montanari et al. [18] mixed 30 % PEG and 70 % poly-methylmethacrylate, and combined with the birch wood to obtain PCES-Wood. The composite material not only showed good heat storage properties, but also showed good light transmission. (4) The improvement of the heat transfer efficiency of PCES-Wood, including carbon-based heat conduction nanoparticles, metal-based heat-conducting nanoparticles and inorganic heat-conducting nanoparticles. For example, Yang et al. [19] impregnated Fe3O4 and myristyl alcohol into wood, and the addition of Fe3O4 nanoparticles improved the photothermal conversion efficiency of the composites. Benefiting from the magnetothermal effect, the wood composite can be heated under an alternating magnetic field.

Our study selected PEG as the PCM to combine with wood to prepare the PCES-Wood. PEG belongs to amphiphilic solid-liquid type PCM, which is soluble in water and in most organic solvents, and has the advantages of good biocompatibility, high enthalpy of phase change and non-toxicity [20,21]. It is allowed to adjust the PCT and the enthalpy of PEG by adjusting the length of the molecular chain. The synthesis idea of PCES-Wood prepared in this study is mainly divided into two parts. (1) Construction of heat storage skeleton of wood. The wood was delignification treatment to open the internal pores, and then maleic anhydride was used to treat the delignified wood with cyclic anhydride to improve the reactivity of the surface. (2) Preparation of thermal conductivity enhanced PCM. In order to prevent the leakage of PEG, glymethacrylate with grafting polymerization function and ethylene glycol dimethacrylic acid with polymerization function of flexible ether chain were mixed with PEG to build a phase change heat storage crosslinking network. For improving the thermal conductivity (TC) efficiency exhibited by the synthetic PCM, we selected the hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) as the TC-enhancing particle to blend with composite. The h-BN refers to a kind of inorganic nonmetallic material, it is thermally conductive and can be well insulated, and it is capable of obtaining higher thermal conductivity at lower filling quantities [22,23]. With a broadband gap of 5.2 eV, h-BN has strong responsiveness to UV light but poorly to visible light [24–26]. For enhancing its response to visible light, GQDs-BN composite thermal conductivity particles were prepared by using graphene graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as graft material. As a new type of carbon nanomaterials, GQDs not only has efficient thermal and electrical conductivity, but also has the advantages of non-toxic and easy doping modification functionalization. In the field of optics, GQDs has adjustable band gaps that extend the wavelength range from the near-infrared region to the visible and deep ultraviolet regions [27–29]. (3) Construction of PCES-Wood. The synthetic PCM with enhanced heat conduction was soaked into the reactive wood heat storage skeleton by vacuum pressure full cell method to obtain PCES-Wood. The chemical properties of the PCES-Wood were compared at different GQDs-BN content. The PCES-Wood prepared in this study was expected to be used in the fields of aerospace, building materials, furniture and electronic components packaging materials, which improves natural woods' temperature regulation function and also provides new ideas for the functional development of natural materials.

Our study used the material of Balsa (with the size of 20 mm × 20 mm × 5 mm). Chemicals used included Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, purity >96 %), sodium sulphite (Na2SO3, purity >98 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, concentration 10 %), γ—Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (KH550, purity >98 %), Graphene quantum dots (GQDs, 5 mg/mL), polyethylene glycol (PEG, average Mn = 800 and Mn = 1000), Maleic anhydride (MAN, purity >98 %), glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDMA), benzoin dimethyl ether (DMPA), hexagonal-Boron Nitride (h-BN, 20 nm ~ 100 nm, TC 180 W/(m·k), which were all analytical pure (Aladdin-Holdings Group).

Balsa delignification removed some lignin and opened the inner channel. The pouring of 500 mL deionized water into the beaker was followed by the addition of NaOH (2.5 mol/L) and Na2SO3 (0.4 mol/L). The solution was heated to 95 °C, and then added with the wood to receive 10 h of cooking. The wood sample underwent 3 h of bleaching in 10 % H2O2 solution for facilitating the further removal of the rest fewer lignin. After the wood template turned white, it received three times of washes and soaking in deionized water. Then the wood received 2 h of cooking at 70 °C in anhydrous ethanol solution. After the complete dehydration, the wood sample was preserved in acetone solution for subsequent use and named as PRE-wood. A 10 % MAN solution was prepared after the dissolution of some drops of pyrimidine in the acetone. The PRE-wood underwent 2 h of reaction in the MAN solution at 80 °C, to obtain the final sample MAN-wood.

50 g BN and 500 mL H2O2 aqueous solution (30 wt%) were poured into the flask. The mixture underwent a few minutes of sonication and 1 h of heating treatment at 80 °C. Then the mixture underwent 4 h of refluxing at 105 °C in vigorous stirring, and was washed by deionized water with two times and vacuum dried to get OH-BN.

The mixture of 50 mL of distilled water and 450 mL of ethanol served for preparing a 95 % ethanol aqueous solution and added with 2.5 wt% KH550 to prepare a KH550 solution. We adjusted the pH of KH550 solution to 3–4 with an aqueous oxalic acid solution and then the solution received 1 h of hydrolyzation at 40 °C. 40 g OH-BN was dispersed in the hydrolysis coupling agent solvent to receive 8 h of heating treatment at 40 °C. At last, the mixture received several times of washes in ethanol and 12 h of dying treatment for obtaining KH550-BN.

10 mL, 30 mL and 50 mL COOH-GQDs dispersion (5 mg/mL) were added into 500 mL deionized water and sonicated for 10 min, respectively. Then, 1 g KH550-BN was added to the COOH-GQDs dispersion and the reaction underwent 2 h of stirring at 30 °C to form the GQDs-BN hybrid. The GQDs-BN hybrid was collected via centrifugation and washed in deionized water several times. Finally, the GQDs-BN hybrid was vacuum dried at 50 °C for 24 h and screened out the best ratio.

(1) The mixture of 6 mLPEG-800 and 14 mL PEG-1000 was melted at 50 °C in a water bath. Then the PEG blend underwent 15 min of stirring at 800–1000 rpm. After that, a cold-heat cycle was performed at a temperature of 0–50 °C and repeated for three times. The wood was then put into the PEG blend to undergo 2 h of cooking at 90 °C. (2) After being cooled to 35 °C, the PEG blend was added with 1 mL GMA, 1 mL EDMA and 0.01 g DMPA for synthesizing a PCES monomer and received 10 min of stirring at 800–1000 rpm. The mixture of GQDs-BN and 10 mL of acetone received 10 min of ultrasonication, followed by being poured into the PCES monomer to receive 15 min of stirring at 800-1000 rpm for synthesizing PCES material with thermal enhancement. The content of GQDs-BN was 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 4 wt%, 6 wt% of the mass of the PCES monomer. Then after being put into PCES material with thermal enhancement, the MAN-Wood underwent 5 h of cooking at 50 °C. After completion, the aluminum foil paper-wrapped sample received 1 h of heating treatment in an oven at 130 °C, and then was exposed to an ultraviolet lamp for 30 min for obtaining a PCES-Wood with thermal enhancement, which was named as GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa. Fig. 1 displays the preparation of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa.

The microstructure of samples were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM) by JSM-6700F at 20 kV voltage and transmission electron microscope (TEM) by JEM-3010 at 200 kV voltage. In the NicoletIS10 Fourier transformation infrared (FTIR) spectrum, the range was 400–4000 cm−1 and the resolution ratio was 0.4 cm−1. The photoelectron spectrum of the sample was measured using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) by XD-3 with a power of 150 W. The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was served by BrukerAXS at 5°/min in a 2θ range of 5°-80°. Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC) and Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) assisted in measuring the thermal property. For DSC, the temperature range was 0–50 °C and the heating and cooling rate was 10 °C/min using Q200. For TGA, the temperature range was 25–800 °C and the heating rate was 10 °C/min using TGA209F3. The light absorption capacity of the sample was tested using a UV visible diffuse reflectance spectrometer by UV-3600, with a wavelength scanning range of 300–800 nm. The deviation from the original quality (M1) to the quality after 4 h of heating (M2) at 80 °C in oven was compared for measuring the solid-liquid phase transition. Then the Digital camera was chosen to measure the shape stability. We placed all samples under the simulated sunlight, and the temperature at various times was recorded by connecting a K-thermocouple to the surface for representing the heat conductivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical properties and dispersibility of GQDs-BN
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a gives the TEM images of BN and GQDs-BN. Comparing with BN, many flaky particles are deposited on GQDs-BN surface, demonstrating the successful deposition of GQDs on BN surface. Fig. 2b displays the XPS full spectra of BN, OH-BN, KH550-BN and GQDs-BN. It can be found that the intensity of the N1s peak and the B1s peak are reduced after BN is modified. The intensity of O1s peak and C1s peak of OH-BN and KH550-BN have increased, indicating the existence of covalent bonding. The intensity of the C1s peak of GQDs-BN decreased again, indicating that there may be a covalent bond between the oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQDs and KH550 surface. After the peaks of C1s, O1s were split, the existence of CO (285.98 eV) bond and CC (284.80 eV) bond of BN may be due to the small amount of CO2 adsorbed by BN (Fig. 2c). The O1s of BN cannot isolate the peaks, demonstrating the less O element content and the absence of chemical bonding (Fig. 2d). There is only the BN (397.98 eV) bond exists for the peak splitting of the N1s of BN (Fig. 2e). After the peak split of C1s of OH-BN, the new peaks of CC(284.18 eV), CH(284.60 eV), COC(286.18 eV) and OCO(288.58 eV) appear (Fig. 3f). Splitting the main peak of O1s (Fig. 2g), OH-BN newly added CO (532.18 eV), CO (531.88 eV). After the split of the main peak of N1s (Fig. 2h), OH-BN newly added NC (397.48 eV), N = (398.50 eV) and a small part of NH (400 eV) bonds. The above results indicate that H2O2 treatment increases the oxygen-containing functional groups and binding sites on the BN surface. After the peak split of C1s of KH550-BN (Fig. 2i), the CC (284.78 eV) peak of KH550-BN is enhanced and the CC (284.38 eV) peak is weakened. The O1s peak cannot fit more chemical bonds (Fig. 2j), indicating that the oxygen-containing functional groups are reduced [30]. The N1s peak fits the CNC (398.08 eV) peak and the NH (397.68 eV) peak (Fig. 2k). It is worth noting that the intensity of the NH peak is greatly increased, indicating that KH550 successfully reacted with the oxygen-containing functional groups on the BN surface and introduced NH2. It can be observed that the intensity of CO (532.28 eV) peak of GQDs-BN increases through splitting the main peaks of C1s and O1s of GQDs-BN (Fig. 2l and m). The intensity of CNC (398.08 eV) peak of GQDs-BN decreases by fitting the peaks of N1s (Fig. 2n), and the bond strength of NH (397.68 eV) further increases. The above results indicate that -COOH of GQDs and -NH2 of KH550-BN have an amide reaction and a peptide bond NHCO is formed, that enables the successful grafting of GQDs to the BN surface in the form of covalent bond. Therefore, it can be inferred that the combination process of GQDs and BN is as follows (Fig. 3).

In Fig. 4a, BN, OH-BN and KH550-BN present characteristic peaks at 1367 cm−1, which is due to the E
2
g vibration in the high-frequency inner layer of BN [31]. However, the characteristic peaks in the Raman spectrum of GQDs-BN disappear and noises appear. This is because GQDs are zero-dimensional nanocarbons with narrow and complex band gaps, which are prone to photofluorescence. When GQDs are loaded on the BN surface, they can strongly absorb photons and convert each absorbed photon into a fluorescent photon, which indirectly indicates the successful coating of GQDs on the BN surface.

Fig. 4b shows the XRD diffraction pattern of BN, OH-BN, KH550-BN and GQDs-BN. All samples show a crystallization peak at 26.6°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane. That is to say, the structure of all the samples do not change and the crystallization is complete [32,33]. The Bragg equation 2dsinθ = nλ (d denotes the interplanar spacing; θ denotes the angle between the incident ray, the reflected line and the reflecting plane; λ is the wavelength; and n is the reflection order number) is used to calculate the interplanar spacing [34], which is 3.3434 Å, 3.3409 Å, 3.3384 Å, 3.3360 Å, 3.3410 Å and 3.3385 Å for BN, OH-BN, KH550-BN, 10 GQDs-BN, 30GQDs-BN and 50GQDs-BN, respectively. The interplanar spacing of modified BN is smaller than the interplanar spacing of BN, indicating that in addition to the existence of GQDs on the surface of BN as a simple substance of C, C atoms may also enter the crystal lattice of BN. The modified BN presents larger diffraction peaks of the (002) crystal plane relative to the BN, indicating that the growth direction of the BN crystal is suppressed with the addition of GQDs and the growth of the (002) crystal plane is relatively promoted.

Fig. 4c shows the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra of BN and GQDs-BN. Unmodified BN has a strong absorption peak near 200 nm, and the corresponding band gap is 6.2 eV, resulted from electron transfer from the valence band to the conduction band. GQDs have strong optical absorption in the ultraviolet and visible light regions. The absorbance of the composite material increases and moves to the visible light region when BN and GQDs are doped. Among them, 30GQDs-BN possesses the largest absorbance in the ultraviolet-visible light region. It is because electron-hole pairs are formed between BN and GQDs and the carriers on the surface of the material increase, thereby increasing the sensing current.

In Fig. 4d that displays the thermal weight loss (TWL) curve of BN and GQDs-BN, the TWL rate of GQDs-BN is always lower than that of BN before 410 °C. This result shows that GQDs successfully adhered to the surface of BN and reduced the absorb water and oxygen-containing functional groups of BN. For GQDs-BN, the TWL between 35 °C and 150 °C is resulted from the adsorbed water evaporation [35]. The TWL between 150 °C and 450 °C is attributed to the molecular chain scission of KH550 and the combustion decomposition of GQDs. At this stage, 50GQDs-BN shows the fastest TWL, indicating that the rate of TWL is positively correlated with the content of GQDs, but the overall thermal stability is still higher than that of BN. After 500 °C, the TWL curve of GQDs-BN tends to be flat, mainly due to the decomposition of residual carbon and part of BN. At 800 °C, the TWL rates of 10GQDs-BN, 30GQDs-BN and 50GQDs-BN are 3.5 %, 3.8 % and 5.6 %, respectively, they all have good thermal stability.

0.5 mg/mL BN and GQDs-BN were dispersed in water and PEG400 (Fig. 5 ), respectively, and their dispersion stability was observed. It can be seen that the BN aqueous solution shows obvious sedimentation after standing for one day, while the GQDs-BN aqueous solution is still relatively turbid after standing for one day. When BN was dispersed in PEG400, delamination occurred on the second day. While GQDs-BN remains suspended in PEG400 for three days. The above results indicate that GQDs-BN has better dispersion stability than BN.

Fig. 6a gives the microstructure of the cross sections of PCES-Wood added with 1 %, 2 %, 4 %, and 6 % of GQDs-BN, respectively. Wood supports PCM as the skeleton. GQDs-BN is coated on the surface of the wood and permeated into little pores, but there is no GQDs-BN in large holes. From the longitudinal section, it can be seen that GQDs-BN is adhered to the cell wall of wood to form a thermal conductivity pathway, and the GQDs-BN content on the cell wall of 6 % GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa is the highest. From the cross section, the PCM penetrates into the wood cell wall and part of the cell cavity. The FTIR spectra regarding 70PEG1000-Balsa, 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa, and 6 % GQDs-BN-PCES @ Balsa after 500 hot and cold and heat cycles are shown in Fig. 6b. In 70PEG1000-Balsa and GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa, the peak at 2870 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration peaks of the methyl and methylene groups in PGMA. The peak at 1100 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration peak in PGMA (CO) [36]. Adding GQDs-BN fails to alter the PGMA structure, indicating that no chemical reaction occurred between GQDs-BN and 70PEG1000-Balsa. The absorption band at 1450 cm−1 becomes stronger, which belongs to the (OH) vibration absorption peak in the carboxylic acids, and this is attributed to the unreacted carboxyl group on the GQDs. After 500 cold and heat cycles, the main characteristic peak of 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa is unchanged, demonstrating PCES-Woods' good heat storage performance.

Fig. 6c is the XRD diffraction spectra of 70PEG1000-Balsa and PCES-Wood with various GQDs-BN contents. All GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa present obvious intensity peaks at 2 θ = 19.2 and 23.4. The diffraction peak of GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa at 2 θ = 26.8 belongs to the (002) crystal surface of GQDs-BN. In summary, GQDs-BN fails to alter the PGMA crystal structure, but binds to PGMA in the form of physical dispersion, which also conforms to the FTIR results.

The TC of Balsa, 70PEG1000-Balsa and the wood with various GQDs-BN contents are shown in Table 1 . Balsa possesses a low TC at 0.03472 W/(m·K). 70PEG1000-Balsa has a TC of 0.3207 W/(m·K), because the wood has a random and slow heat transfer as a kind of amorphous polymer. 70PEG1000-Balsa fills abundant crystalline polymers, and the heat transfer primarily relies on the crystal lattice vibration. Specific to crystalline polymers that have neat lattice arrangements, the thermal propagation in the lattice is similar to the waves. The increase in GQDs-BN packing content is accompanied by the gradual increase in the material TC. With the filling amount of GQDs-BN reaching 6 wt%, the composite system exhibits a TC of 0.3916 W/(m·K). The GQDs-BN particles contact with each other for creating a good thermal conduction pathway inside the wood.

Fig. 7a and b display the DSC curves of GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa, and Table 2 gives the phase transition temperature (PTT) and phase transition enthalpy (PTE) of the PCES-Wood with various GQDs-BN contents. All samples possess high melting and solidification enthalpies, that is to say, the lower GQDs-BN content does not reduce the enthalpy value of the PCES-Wood. Practically, the supercooling degree and the thermal storage effect exhibited by PCES-Wood should also be significantly considered. Fig. 7c shows the comparison diagram of supercooling and heat loss rate. According to Fig. 7d, 1 % GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa shows the least supercooling, but the largest heat loss rate. 4 % GQ-BN-PCES-Balsa shows the largest supercooling, but the least heat loss rate. However, the supercooling degree of 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa is only 0.67 °C higher than that of 1 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa, and the heat loss rate is only 1 %, so it can be used as the preferred sample.

The cycle performance of PCES-Wood determines its service life. The comparison of the original DSC data with that after the heat and cold cycle helps to understand the cycle performance of PCES-Wood. Our study selected 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa as the research object. Fig. 8a and b display the DSC curve before and after the 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa cooling cycle. Table 3 shows the PTT and PTE before and after the hot and cold cycle. The results show that after 200 cold-heat cycles, there is only little peak type change of 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa, with the melting point and cooling point reduced by 2.28 °C and 4.55 °C, respectively. The melting enthalpy increased from 114.90 J/g to 124.50 J/g, and the solidification enthalpy decreased from 117.70 J/g to 113.80 J/g. The little change proves the good service life of the PCES-Wood.

Fig. 8c and d display the TGA and DTG curves of PCES-Wood with various GQDs-BN contents. Table 3 shows the temperature points of the sample at various weight loss rates and the final remaining mass percentage. For all the samples, the corresponding DTG curve only has one peak, and the largest weight loss rate occurs at around 400 °C. 70PEG1000-Balsa possesses a distinct weightlessness step between 30 and 100 °C for the evaporation of the bound water. However, there is no drastic weight loss steps of GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa in the range of 30–100 °C, indicating that GQDs-BN increase the heat stability of PCES-Wood, meanwhile, increasing numbers of physical cross-linking points formed between GQDs-BN and PGMA enhances the molecular interaction force, further enhancing the thermal stability. All the samples slow down after 400 °C, that demonstrates the completion of the thermal decomposition regarding PGMA and wood.

Fig. 9a gives the UV–vis absorption spectra of the PCES-Wood with various GQDs-BN contents. All samples show light absorption capability in the UV segment and poor absorption capability in the visible segment and infrared segment. In the UV segment, 6 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa has the largest light absorption capacity, followed by 4 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa, 1 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa and 2 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa, indicating the positive relationship between the UV adsorbability of GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa and the GQDs-BN content.

The energy utilization rate depends on the heat storage rate of the PCM. Fig. 9b shows the light-to-heat conversion curve of the PCES-Wood, at the beginning of the light-based irradiation, there is a significant and rapid increase in surface temperature of GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa. After 1137s of light irradiation, the surface temperatures of 1 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa, 2 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa and 4 % GQ-BN-PCES @ Balsa are all greater than the surface temperatures of 70PEG1000-Balsa, hence, GQDs-BN can enhance the TC efficiency exhibited by PCES-Wood. Balsa has a faster heating rate than 70PEG1000-Balsa, but the maximum surface temperature of Balsa can only reach 12.9 °C, because Balsa has less heat storage capacity and the heat is rapidly lost. During the cooling phase, the cooling rate of Balsa reaches the maximum.

The leakage rate characterizes the solid-liquid phase transformation behavior, reflecting the leakage situation of the PCM at high temperature and assisting in the selection of the optimal ratio. The experimental condition of this study was to heat the sample at 80 °C for 4 h. Fig. 10a displays the leakage before and after temperature rise of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa. Difference measurement before and after temperature rise helps to obtain the leakage quantity (Table 4 ). After being heated at 80 °C for 4 h, GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa develops a slight leak, because the wood crystallizing water are evaporated and PGMA is melted. Nevertheless, all samples have leakage <0.1 g, and GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa has a leakage rate of only 0.70 % because PGMA and GQDs-BN interweave with each other for obtaining a 3D network structure within the wood [37], thereby limiting PGMA leakage. In addition, the thermal conductivity layer formed by GQDs-BN on the wood surface exhibits a strong thermal stability and is capable of protecting PGMA encapsulation.

The study adopted the high temperature-pressure method for preparing the PCES-Wood taking delignified balsa wood as the heat storage skeleton. We designed a composite nanoparticle composed of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and BN as the thermal conductivity filler for achieving the TC efficiency of the whole system. The XPS shows that -COOH in GQDs and -NH2 of KH550-BN undergo amide reaction and form peptide bond -NH-CO-, and GQDs is successfully grafted to BN surface in the form of covalent bond. GQDs-BN shows strong light absorption ability in both ultraviolet and visible segments. The dispersion in organic solvents of GQDs-BN is greater than that of BN. The penetration of GQDs-BN into some lumen of the PCES-Wood can improve the thermal stability. When the GQDs-BN content reaches 6 %, the TC of the PCES-Wood will be the maximum data as 0.3916 W/(m·K) for the GQDs-BN forms a good heat-conducting pathway inside the wood. GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa also shows good heat storage capacity, among which 6 % GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa exhibits the smallest supercooling degree and heat loss rate, and the melting and solidification enthalpies reach 114.90 J/g and 113.70 J/g, respectively. The temperature of phase transformation is in the range of 20.29 °C and 36.29 °C according with the comfortable human body temperature range. The surface temperature of GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa presents a fast increase and exhibits a persistent heat storage capacity under low temperature conditions. After 200 cold to heat cycle tests, the phase change enthalpy regarding GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa remains high, and the PCT still accords with the comfortable human body temperature range, demonstrating the good durability of the PCES-Wood with GQDs-BN as thermal conductivity filler. All the GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa shows a slight leak, and 6 % GQ-BN-PCES@Balsa has a leakage rate of only 0.70 %. The study expands the potential of wood application in the field of temperature regulation.
Yanchen Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology. Qi Lu: Writing – original draft. Jiawei Yang: Writing – review & editing. Weiwei He: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Postdoctoral Innovation Talents Support Program (BX20230185), the Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (3244042).
